title: Measures of Spread (range, variance, standard deviation)

quiz_questions.question_text: The range of a data set is calculated by:

options.text: Subtracting the smallest value from the largest value
Dividing the sum by the count
Finding the middle value
Squaring all the values and adding them

quiz_questions.explanation: Range = maximum value − minimum value. It is the simplest measure of spread.

quiz_questions.question_text: The range of {12, 18, 25, 30, 45} is:
Variance measures:

options.text: The center of the data
The average squared deviation from the mean
The most frequent value
The difference between the largest and smallest values

quiz_questions.explanation: Variance is the average of the squared deviations from the mean, quantifying how spread out the data are.

quiz_questions.question_text: The standard deviation is:

options.text: The square of the variance
The square root of the variance
The same as the range
Always negative

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard deviation = √variance. It returns the spread measure to the original units of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the data set {2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9}, the mean is 5. What is the population variance?
If the standard deviation of a data set is 0, this means:

options.text: All values are the same
The data set is empty
All values are negative
The mean is 0

quiz_questions.explanation: A standard deviation of 0 means every value equals the mean, so there is no variability at all.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do we use n − 1 (instead of n) in the denominator when calculating sample variance?

options.text: To make the variance smaller
To correct for the bias of underestimating population variance from a sample
To make the calculations simpler
Because sample sizes must be odd

quiz_questions.explanation: Using n−1 (Bessel's correction) provides an unbiased estimate of the population variance when working with a sample.

quiz_questions.question_text: If every value in a data set is multiplied by 3, the standard deviation will:

options.text: Stay the same
Be multiplied by 3
Be multiplied by 9
Be divided by 3

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiplying all values by a constant c multiplies the standard deviation by |c|. Variance is multiplied by c².

quiz_questions.question_text: Which data set has the greater range: Set A = {10, 20, 30} or Set B = {14, 15, 16}?

options.text: Set B
Set A
They have the same range
Cannot be determined

quiz_questions.explanation: Range of A = 30−10 = 20. Range of B = 16−14 = 2. Set A has a much greater range.

quiz_questions.question_text: Adding a constant to every value in a data set will change the standard deviation by:

options.text: The same constant
Nothing; the standard deviation stays the same
Double the constant
The square of the constant

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding a constant shifts all values equally, so the deviations from the new mean remain unchanged.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the range of a data set?

options.text: The difference between the largest and smallest values
The average of all values
The middle value
The sum of all squared deviations

quiz_questions.explanation: The range is the simplest measure of spread: maximum minus minimum.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is variance?

options.text: The average of the squared deviations from the mean
The square root of the standard deviation
The most common value in the data
The range divided by 2

quiz_questions.explanation: Variance quantifies spread by averaging the squared differences between each data point and the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is standard deviation?

options.text: The square root of the variance, expressed in the same units as the original data
The square of the range
The average value of the data
The difference between Q3 and Q1

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard deviation is the square root of variance, bringing the spread measure back to the original measurement units.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'deviation' mean in statistics?

options.text: The difference between a data value and the mean
The total number of data points
A graphical display of data
The probability of an event

quiz_questions.explanation: A deviation is the signed distance of a data value from the mean: value − mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'degrees of freedom' mean in the context of sample variance?

options.text: The number of data values that are free to vary after the mean is fixed, typically n − 1
The total number of data values in the sample
The number of different variables measured
The size of the population

quiz_questions.explanation: Once the sample mean is known, only n−1 values can vary freely. This concept explains why we divide by n−1 for sample variance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'measure of spread' mean?

options.text: A statistic that describes how dispersed or scattered the data values are
A statistic that identifies the center of the data
The type of graph used to display data
A method of collecting data

quiz_questions.explanation: Measures of spread (also called measures of variability or dispersion) describe how much the data values differ from one another.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Bessel's correction?

options.text: A method for removing outliers
The use of n − 1 instead of n in the denominator of the sample variance formula to produce an unbiased estimate
A technique for finding the median
A graphing method

quiz_questions.explanation: Bessel's correction divides by n−1 rather than n when computing sample variance, correcting the systematic underestimate of population variance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'population standard deviation' mean, compared to 'sample standard deviation'?

options.text: They are identical calculations
Population standard deviation uses N in the denominator (all members); sample standard deviation uses n−1 to correct for estimation bias
Sample standard deviation is always larger than population standard deviation
Population standard deviation can only be used for census data

quiz_questions.explanation: Population σ divides the sum of squared deviations by N; sample s divides by n−1 to give an unbiased estimate of σ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dispersion' mean in statistics?

options.text: The degree to which data values are spread out from the center
The type of sampling used
A method of organizing data into categories
The shape of a distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: Dispersion (also called variability or spread) describes how far apart data values are from the mean and from each other.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the sum of squared deviations (SS)?

options.text: The total of all data values squared
The sum of (each value minus the mean) squared
The square root of the variance
The range squared

quiz_questions.explanation: SS = Σ(xᵢ − x̄)². Dividing SS by N gives population variance; dividing by n−1 gives sample variance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher gives a quiz to two classes. Class A scores: 70, 75, 78, 80, 82. Class B scores: 50, 65, 78, 90, 100. Both classes have the same mean (77). Which class has greater variability, and how can you tell?

options.text: Class A, because its scores are higher
Class B, because its range is 50 compared to Class A's range of 12
They have identical variability since the means are equal
Class A, because its lowest score is higher

quiz_questions.explanation: Class A range = 82−70 = 12. Class B range = 100−50 = 50. Despite equal means, Class B's scores are far more spread out.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quality inspector measures the diameter of 5 bolts (mm): 10.1, 10.0, 10.0, 9.9, 10.0. Calculate the population standard deviation.

options.text: 0.1 mm
Approximately 0.063 mm
0.2 mm

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor tracks monthly returns (%) for two stocks over 6 months. Stock X: 2, 3, 2, 4, 3, 4. Stock Y: −5, 10, −3, 15, 1, 12. Both average 3% per month. Which stock is riskier, and why?

options.text: Stock X, because lower returns are always riskier
Stock Y, because its standard deviation is much larger, indicating greater volatility around the same average return
They are equally risky because they have the same mean
Neither is risky because both averages are positive

quiz_questions.explanation: Stock X's returns cluster tightly around 3% (low SD), while Stock Y swings wildly from −5% to 15% (high SD). Greater standard deviation indicates higher risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sample of 4 daily high temperatures (°F) is: 60, 65, 70, 75. What is the sample variance (using n − 1)?

A data analyst finds that a company's daily sales figures have a mean of $500 and a standard deviation of $120. If the company converts all sales records to euros at a fixed rate of 0.90 euros per dollar, what will the new standard deviation be?

options.text: $120 (unchanged)
108 euros
133.33 euros
14,400 euros

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiplying all values by 0.90 multiplies the standard deviation by 0.90: 120 × 0.90 = 108 euros.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher measures reaction times (ms) for 6 participants: 200, 210, 215, 220, 225, 350. She notices the range is 150 ms but suspects the 350 ms value is an outlier. If she removes it, the range drops to 25 ms. What does this illustrate about the range as a measure of spread?

options.text: The range is always the best measure of spread
The range is highly sensitive to outliers because it depends only on the two extreme values
The range is resistant to outliers
The outlier doesn't affect any measure of spread

quiz_questions.explanation: The range dropped from 150 ms to 25 ms by removing one value, showing that the range depends entirely on the extremes and is very sensitive to outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: A fitness trainer records the number of push-ups completed by 5 clients: 20, 22, 25, 23, 20. She then adds 10 push-ups to each client's count as a bonus. What happens to the standard deviation?

options.text: It increases by 10
It doubles
It stays the same, because adding a constant does not change spread
It decreases by 10

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding a constant to every value shifts the entire distribution without changing the distances between values, so the standard deviation is unchanged.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two pizza delivery services report delivery times. Service A: mean = 30 min, SD = 3 min. Service B: mean = 30 min, SD = 10 min. A customer wants the most consistent delivery time. Which service should they choose and why?

options.text: Service B, because higher SD means faster deliveries
Service A, because its smaller SD (3 min) means delivery times are more consistent around 30 minutes
Either service, because they have the same mean
Neither, because SD doesn't measure consistency

quiz_questions.explanation: A smaller standard deviation means values cluster more tightly around the mean. Service A's 3-minute SD indicates much more consistent delivery times.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data set of exam scores has a population variance of 225. What is the population standard deviation?
quiz_questions.explanation: Standard deviation = √variance = √225 = 15.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company tests two batches of a drug for potency (mg). Batch 1: 98, 99, 100, 101, 102 (mean = 100, SD ≈ 1.58). Batch 2: 85, 92, 100, 108, 115 (mean = 100, SD ≈ 12.25). Which batch meets stricter quality control standards, and what measure supports this?

options.text: Batch 2, because it has more extreme values showing thorough testing
They are equally good since both have the same mean
Batch 1, because its much smaller standard deviation (≈1.58 vs ≈12.25) shows potency values are tightly clustered around the target
Neither batch meets standards because the range is nonzero

quiz_questions.explanation: Both batches average 100 mg, but Batch 1's tiny SD shows consistent potency near the target, while Batch 2's large SD indicates unacceptable variability.
